text: This video shows how transperineal operative access can be used to assist laparoscopic completion protectomy and parastomal hernia repair in a male patient.
The man had previously had an emergency total colectomy and end ileostomy for ulcerative colitis.
The surgeon begins the laparoscopic mobilization of the residual rectal stump, preparing for the completion operation.
most especially in its posterior and lateral aspect.
The surgeon uses transperineal access to mobilize the lower rectum, focusing on the anterior aspect, as part of the laparoscopic procedure.
Following the suture closure and circumferential mobilization of the anus, the surgeon places a single port access device, the Gel Point Path by Applied Medical, into the perineal wound.
The video shows the suture-closed anorectum, providing a clear view for circumferential mobilization of the rectum.
The surgeon continues the dissection to connect with the laparoscopic mobilization of the residual rectal stump, preparing to remove the specimen through the perineal access.
The surgeon uses point cautery to achieve excellent hemostasis, controlling bleeding effectively during this stage of the operation.
The laparoscopic view shows the transperineal approach, with visible gas bubbles indicating the connection between the dissection from below and above.
After the specimen is mobilized and removed, the surgeon replaces the gel point path device into the perineal access, providing a clear view of the abdominal compartment.
The surgeon mobilizes the endoleostomy, allowing for the placement of a dyna mesh around the stoma. The small bowel and mesh are then returned into the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon places Alexa's wound protector into the stoma incision site, securing the area for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon positions the mesh in a well-oriented manner and secures it in a double layer within the surgical field.
On the extracorporeal side, a glove is placed to ensure a gas seal around the abdominal incision site, visible in the surgical field.
so that a stable pneumoperitoneum can be achieved throughout the operation.
Two working instruments are visible, inserted through the transperitoneal single port access device, facilitating the surgical procedure.
The surgeon prepares to tack the mesh perpendicular to the abdominal wall, checking its position to ensure proper alignment.
The surgeon uses digital indentation to securely place the mesh against the abdominal wall, ensuring it is properly oriented and tacked in a double layer.
The surgeon places tucks circumferentially around the mesh with excellent visualization, using a right-angled approach to ensure secure attachment to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon places the tacker perpendicular to the abdominal wall using the transperitoneal single port access device, contrasting with the oblique placement seen with conventional laparoscopic port sites.
The surgeon irrigates the transperineal wound and pelvic cavity with Betadine solution before replacing the transperineal access device.
The surgeon uses subumbilical trocar access for laparoscopic mobilization of the upper rectum, which can be incorporated into the mesh repair during the hernia procedure.
The surgeon removes the wound protector after placing the initial row of tacks circumferentially, then proceeds to place an inner circle of tacks around the mesh within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon removes the gel point path and proceeds to close the perineal wound using standard suturing techniques.
There were no interoperative difficulties during the case and the patient had a normal post-operative course.
He remains well now, twelve months after the operation and has had neither recurrence of his parastomal hernia nor any perineal wound healing complications.

We've done this in an additional two patients also having barostoma hernia at the time of their completion proctectomy with no adverse complications in the intraoperative, early or intermediate postoperative.
